feedback.text: As your act seems to be close enough i reckon i could explain my personal view on the situation. I have been playing PC games for a while (since 1'992) and whilst i was always vaguely aware of there being rather shady "you do not own these" fine print on some more abrasive companies it did not quite click with me how fragile the market is and how much it is tilted against costumers. games, even massive hits like no one lives forever and its sequel or test drive unlimited one and two could become unavailable over time in spite of digital distribution making it easy to have infinite ability to just keep the game ready for players to install whenever. Now they've become unavailable in peculiar and diverse ways - one is simply not sold where as the TDU/2 dose no longer have functionality to be played due to owners of the brand choosing to shut down any and all support - no planning or care fr future of users what so ever. I am deeply disturbed by this apparently increasingly affecting games that have even single player only modes. and would wish to have something like "stop killing games" initiative become a serious consideration to those in power. Afterall if you had bought a VHS or DVD of say the 1960s Italian job or Danger Diabolik, or watch out we're mad you will own it as long as you want, without any input form studio past point of sale. you can watch it as many times as tape can take and provided it is treated well will last decades. I want similar for gamers for games are art as proven by countless titles across decades (ever heard of blacksad, genesis noire, or yes another one of those no longer avalablee games: the neverhood?), it is artform that speaks to its audience in manner comapnies do not appretiate. And to that end i plead for addition of another point into the digital fairness act: ownership of digital entertainment (including non live videos, music, games etc.) should be not token for access (unless specified example: LIVE concert) but right to play and replay. this offcourse dose not mean the game / movie / music has to be maintained forever that would be silly. leaving it in state where fans can pick up the slack and do some patchwork to keep it compatible is sufficient. some fans for example do maintain private servers for TDU2. too long and you did not read?: game sales are not treated as they should be i plead for you to add to your digital fairness act a case for digitally owned games / movies / music (etc.) to be upon point of support from publisher ending in state where fans can keep the nescesary services alive. i have purchsed a game and not temporary pass.
